elements.sent-2411-004: Use the plain abstract command if the abstract is also to appear in the printed version of the book.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we apply the framework of optimal transport to the formulation of optimal design problems.
elements.sent-2411-006: By considering the Wasserstein space as a set of design variables, we associate each probability measure with a shape configuration of a material in some ways.
elements.sent-2411-007: In particular, we focus on connections between differentials on the Wasserstein space and sensitivities in the standard setting of shape and topology optimization in order to regard the optimization procedure of those problems as gradient flows on the Wasserstein space.
elements.sent-2411-008: Shape and topology optimization are optimization problems to determine the material design and have been widely studied from the viewpoints of both mathematical theory and applications.
elements.sent-2411-009: They are formulated as minimization problems for objective functionals defined on a space of sets.
elements.sent-2411-010: Shape optimization refers to the case where the space of sets consists of ones sharing the same topology, which means we only determine the shape of the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-011: On the other hand, topology optimization incorporates the optimization of the configuration of materials including their topology, which deals with the most general setting of material design problems.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this case, the objective functional is defined on a space of sets without topological constraints and our aim is to find the minimizer of the functional to determine the optimal design.
elements.sent-2411-013: Although the mathematical formulations of them seem to be challenging as we need to deal with the optimization problem on the space of shapes, which is thought to be a complicated infinite dimensional space, there have been several methods to formulate and implement the optimization.
elements.sent-2411-014: Those methods are roughly classified according to how to describe the material.
elements.sent-2411-015: Let $D \subset \mathbb{R ^d$ be a fixed convex domain.}
elements.sent-2411-016: We suppose that we are given a function $\Omega \mapsto \mathcal{J (\Omega)$ defined on a set $\mathcal{O _{ad}$ composed of subsets in $D$.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: One standard method to find a minimizer of $\mathcal{J}$ is to introduce the level set function defined on $D$ corresponding to configuration of material $\Omega \in \mathcal{O _{ad}$ in such a way that \begin{equation} \phi > 0\ on\ \Omega, \phi = 0\ on\ \partial \Omega, \phi < 0\ on\ D \backslash \Omega. \end{equation} Then we rewrite the objective function as a function of $\phi$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In order to find a minimizer of the objective function, typically we need to calculate a direction in which the objective function decreases.
elements.sent-2411-019: Since in general the objective function is not Fr\'echet differentiable with respect to the level set function, the direction is often found by employing an alternative sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-020: In the context of shape optimization, the level set function is updated by the Hamilton-Jacobi equation.
elements.sent-2411-021: In nice cases, the shape derivative can be obtained as a vector field on the boundary of the current shape and it can describe the movement of the frontier.
elements.sent-2411-022: On the other hand, in order to grasp the sensitivity against the change of the topology, the topological derivative has been calculated in some cases in \cite{Sokolowski99 \cite{Garreau01 \cite{Feijioo03 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The topological derivative is a sensitivity against the creation of a small hole inside the current domain.
elements.sent-2411-024: This sensitivity has been incorporated in the process of optimization in \cite{Allaire06 which considered the shape and topological derivative separately.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In \cite{Yamada10 , the method to updated the level set function by the reaction-diffusion equation has been introduced and the topological derivative has been used as a substitution for the gradient of objective functions in the usual sense.}
elements.sent-2411-026: See Definitions \ref{shapeder and \ref{topologyder for details of these sensitivities.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Another cerebrated formulation of topology optimization is called the density method.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this method, we consider a nonnegative function $\rho \in L^{\infty (D)$ and regard it as a distribution of material.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Then we optimize the functional defined on $L^{\infty (D)$ instead of the original one defined on the set of shapes.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this case, we can find a descent direction by calculating the Fr\'echet differential on $L^{\infty (D)$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: It is known that in typical cases the differential can be explicitly calculating by the adjoint method (See Example \ref{exElasticity .) In this paper, we focus on methods to regard shape and topology optimization as optimization problems on infinite dimensional manifolds which consists of configurations on a fixed domain.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Formulations of shape optimization from this viewpoint has been developed recently.
elements.sent-2411-033: In \cite{MM06 \cite{MM07 , several Riemannian metrics on the space of shapes have been investigated.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In general, the structure of a Riemannian manifold determines the gradient of functions defined on the space, which leads us to formulate optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-035: Based on those Riemannian structures, the shape optimization has been regarded as the optimization problem on the space of shapes and the relation between the gradient with respect to the Riemannian metric and the shape derivative of objective functions was clarified in \cite{Arguillere14 \cite{Schulz14 \cite{Welker21 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: On the other hand, we can also choose the Wasserstein space, which consists of probability measures on the base space, as a space which can describe the distribution of materials.
elements.sent-2411-037: By virtue of the Otto calculus, the Wasserstein space can be thought to have a structure of an infinite dimensional Riemannian manifold, namely, we can construct the notion of smooth curves, tangent spaces, differentials and gradient flows on the Wasserstein space.
elements.sent-2411-038: In particular, we can formulate the optimization procedure on the Wasserstein space by the gradient flow associated with a functional defined on the space.
elements.sent-2411-039: The aim of this article is to reformulate shape and topology optimization as optimization problems on Wasserstein spaces and reconstruct sensitivities of objective functions from the differential structure on those spaces.
elements.sent-2411-040: This point of view can be seen in \cite{Buttazo01 which focuses on the shape optimization problem with a mass constraint and writes down the systems which optimal distributions of materials need to satisfy.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In \cite{MM07 referred to above, the comparison of the Riemannian metric on the space of shapes and the Wasserstein distance is also mentioned by making the boundary of shapes correspond to the uniform distribution on the boundary.}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, our formulation is closer to the one in \cite{Buttazo01 and we incorporate the change of the topology.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section \ref{opt , we propose the two kinds of formulation.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The first one in Subsection \ref{Formulation1 is to regard the supports of measures as shapes of a material.}
elements.sent-2411-045: As readers can see later in Propositions \ref{WassShape1 , \ref{WassShape2 and \ref{WassTop , in this case we can easily check that the shape and topological derivatives can be realized as directional derivatives or higher-order differentials along some absolutely continuous curves on the Wasserstein space.}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Our second formulation in Subsection \ref{Formulation2 consists in the density method but we consider updating the distribution of material by the gradient flow on the Wasserstein distance.}
elements.sent-2411-047: We will see in Proposition \ref{WassDens that the direction to update is given by the gradient of the Fr\'echet differential on $L^{\infty (\mathbb{R ^d)$ in nice cases.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Consequently this method to update turned out to be close to the level set method updated by Hamilton-Jacobi equation.
elements.sent-2411-049: The outline of this paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-050: In Section \ref{transport , we recall some basic settings and facts regarding the theory of optimal transport.}
elements.sent-2411-051: We extract only a few contents which seem to be crucial in this paper from the tremendous theory of optimal transport.
elements.sent-2411-052: In most parts of this section, we refer to \cite{AGS21 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: In Section \ref{opt , we suggest two types of formulations of the shape and topology optimization as mentioned above and reconstruct some sensitivities of objective functions as differentials on the Wasserstein space.}
elements.sent-2411-054: First we recall the basic setting for optimal transport problems.

elements.sent-2411-074: Then we can easily check that for every $\psi(t,x) \in C^{\infty _0((0,T)\times \mathbb{R ^d)$, it holds that \begin{equation}\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \psi (t,x) \partial_t \rho_t(x)\, dxdt =-\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \partial_t\psi (t,x)\rho_t (x)\, dxdt =-\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \partial_t \psi (t,\Phi_t(x)) \rho (x)\, dxdt =-\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \left( \frac{d}{dt} \psi (t,\Phi_t(x)) - \nabla_x \psi (t, x) \cdot \theta (x) \right)\rho(x) \, dxdt =-\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \psi (t,x) div\left(\rho \theta \right)(x)\, dx.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \psi (t,x) \partial_t \rho_t(x)\, dxdt =-\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \partial_t\psi (t,x)\rho_t (x)\, dxdt =-\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \partial_t \psi (t,\Phi_t(x)) \rho (x)\, dxdt =-\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \left( \frac{d}{dt} \psi (t,\Phi_t(x)) - \nabla_x \psi (t, x) \cdot \theta (x) \right)\rho(x) \, dxdt =-\int_0^T\int_{R^d} \psi (t,x) div\left(\rho \theta \right)(x)\, dx.\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-087: To begin with, we recall the basic setting for the shape and topology optimization.
elements.sent-2411-088: We denote the set of all open subsets in $\mathbb{R ^d$ by $\mathcal{O}$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: In many cases, $\mathcal{J}$ is of the form as follows: We suppose that we are given a system described by a PDE on an open subset $\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Let $H$ be a Hilbert space in which solutions of the system live.
elements.sent-2411-093: Let $a_{\Omega}$ be a bounded coercive symmetric bilinear form on $H$ depending on $\Omega \in \mathcal{O _{ad}$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: In the same way, let $l_{\Omega}$ be a bounded linear functional on $H$ depending on $\Omega \in \mathcal{O _{ad}$.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Then there exists a unique $u_{\Omega \in H$ satisfying \begin{equation}\label{governingEq} a_{\Omega}(u_{\Omega},v)=\langle l_{\Omega}, v \rangle \ for all\ v\in H\end{equation} by Lax-Milgram's theorem.}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\label{governingEq} a_{\Omega}(u_{\Omega},v)=\langle l_{\Omega}, v \rangle \ for all\ v\in H\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-096: Let $J \colon \mathcal{O _{ad \times H \to \mathbb{R}$ and suppose that the functional $\mathcal{J \colon \mathcal{O _{ad \to \mathbb{R}$ is given by \begin{equation}J(\Omega)=J(\Omega, u_{\Omega}).\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: In order to apply the gradient descent method to the functional $\mathcal{J}$, we need to consider the perturbation of a domain in $\mathcal{O _{ad}$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: One natural way to give a perturbation is to take a vector field $\theta \colon \mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R ^d$ and consider the image $(\mathrm{Id + \theta)(\Omega)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Here this procedure needs the condition for $\mathcal{O _{ad}$ and a space of vector fields $\Theta_{ad}$ such that $\mathcal{O _{ad}$ contains all the perturbated domain $(\mathrm{Id + \theta)(\Omega)$ for $\Omega \in \mathcal{O _{ad}$ and all small enough $\theta$ in terms of a suitable norm on $\Theta_{ad}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: For instance, if we suppose that $\mathcal{O _{ad}$ contains $C^k$-domains, one can set \begin{equation}\Theta_{ad} =C^{k,\infty}(R^d;R^d) =\left\{ \theta \in C^k(R^d; R^d)\ ;\ \| \theta \|_{C^k}<\infty \right\}\end{equation} as a set of vector fields, where \begin{equation}\| \theta \|_{C^k}= \sup_{|\alpha|\leq k}\| \partial_{\alpha} \theta \|_{L^{\infty}(R^d)}\end{equation} and $\alpha$ is a multi index (See Remark 4.1 of \cite{Allaire21 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Now we recall the sensitivity of the functional regarding the above perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-102: A function $\mathcal{J \colon \mathcal{O _{ad \to \mathbb{R}$ is said to be shape-differentiable at $\Omega \in \mathcal{O _{ad}$ if the functional \begin{equation}\theta \mapsto J((Id+\theta)(\Omega))\end{equation} defined on $\theta \in W^{1,\infty (\mathbb{R ^d ; \mathbb{R ^d)$ is Fr\'echet differentiable at $\theta=0$, namely, there exists a bounded linear functional $d_S\mathcal{J (\Omega)$ on $W^{1,\infty (\mathbb{R ^d;\mathbb{R ^d)$ such that \begin{equation}J((Id+\theta)(\Omega))=J(\Omega)+\langle d_SJ(\Omega), \theta \rangle + o(\theta),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\frac{o(\theta)}{\| \theta \|_{W^{1,\infty}(R^d ; R^d)}} \to 0\ as\ \| \theta \|_{W^{1,\infty}(R^d ; R^d)} \to 0.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: In this case the functional $d_S\mathcal{J (\Omega)$ is called the shape derivative of $\mathcal{J}$ at $\Omega$.}
elements.sent-2411-104: In the process of the shape optimization, we need to calculate the shape derivative of the objective function and find a vector field which decreases the value of it by the deformation of the domain.
elements.sent-2411-105: It is known that in typical nice cases the shape derivative can be obtained in the form of \begin{equation}\langle d_SJ(\Omega), \theta \rangle = \int_{\partial \Omega} g \langle \theta, n\rangle \, dvol_{\partial \Omega},\end{equation} where $g \in L^1(\partial \Omega)$ and $\mathbf{n}$ is the outward normal unit vector field.
elements.sent-2411-106: Since the above deformation is homeomorphic, theoretically the topology of the domain does not change in the process of optimization.
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus in order to incorporate changes of topology and implement the optimization including the the topology of the material, we need to employ other sensitivities.
elements.sent-2411-108: Here we recall the topological sensitivity defined through the perturbation by the creation of small holes.
elements.sent-2411-110: A function $\mathcal{J \colon \mathcal{O _{ad \to \mathbb{R}$ is said to admit a topological derivative at $\Omega \in \mathcal{O _{ad}$ if there exists a strictly increasing continuous function $r \colon [0,\infty) \to [0,\infty)$ such that the limit \begin{equation}\lim_{\ep \to 0} \frac{J(\Omega \backslash \overline{(z + \ep B)})-J(\Omega)}{r(\ep)}\end{equation} exists for every $z\in \Omega$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The limit is called the topological derivative for the hall $B$ with a rate $r$ and denoted by $d_T\mathcal{J (\Omega,B,z)$.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The rate $r$ depends on the functional, but we focus on the typical case where $B$ is the unit ball $B_1(0)$ centered at the origin in $\mathbb{R ^d$ and $r(\ep)=|B_{\ep (0)|$.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: In this case we simply call the function $z\mapsto \mathcal{J (\Omega, B_1(0),|B_{\cdot (0)|)$ the topological derivative at $\Omega$ and denote it by $d_T\mathcal{J (\Omega,z)$.}}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Formulation based on the level set method

elements.sent-2411-222: Let $\Gamma_0$, $\Gamma_1$ be two disjoint $d-1$-dimensional submanifolds in $\mathbb{R ^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Let $H_0^1(\mathbb{R ^d \backslash \Gamma_0; \mathbb{R ^d)$ be the Sobolev space on $\mathbb{R ^d \backslash \Gamma_0$ valued in $\mathbb{R ^d$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: For $u \in H_0^1(\mathbb{R ^d \backslash \Gamma_0; \mathbb{R ^d)$, let $\sigma (u)$, $\ep(u)$ be the stress and strain tensors respectively, which are defined by \begin{equation}\ep(u) =\frac{1}{2}(\nabla u + (\nabla u)^T), \sigma(u) :=2\mu \ep(u)+\lambda tr(\ep(u))I_d\end{equation} for positive constants $\mu$ and $\lambda$.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: For positive $\rho \in L^{\infty (\mathbb{R ^d)$, consider the following system of elasticity for the displacement $u=u_{\rho}$: \begin{equation} -div\left( b(\rho) \sigma(u) \right) + \delta u = f\ in\ R^d \backslash \Gamma, \sigma(u)\bn=g\ on\ \Gamma_1, u=0\ on\ \Gamma_0, \end{equation} where $b \colon [0,\infty) \to (0,\infty)$ be a smooth positive function with a positive infimum, $f \in C^{\infty (\mathbb{R ^d; \mathbb{R ^d)$, $g \colon \Gamma_1 \to \mathbb{R ^d$ is a smooth function and $\delta >0$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-227: This is supposed to provide the deformation of the material with the mass constraint.
elements.sent-2411-228: Compared with the formulation in subsection \ref{Formulation1 , the gradient in the formulation in subsection \ref{Formulation2 can incorporates the change of topology and we do not need to consider the higher order differential.}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Moreover, we can relatively easily derive the sensitivity based on the sensitivity in the density method.
elements.sent-2411-230: However, we still need the additional penalization on the density in order to make the relaxed problem for $\wt{\cJ}$ a good approximation of the original problem defined on $\mathcal{O _{ad}$.}
